text: Marcus Aurelius ruled the Roman Empire while waging an inner campaign most of his subjects never knew existed. In the Meditations — written as private self-reminders, never meant for publication — he returns obsessively to a single question: am I acting in accordance with reason and virtue, or am I being swept along by appetite? He calls the undisciplined mind a 'puppet jerked by every desire.' Virtue, for Marcus, meant acting as the rational, social nature of a human being demands — not pursuing what feels good, but doing what is genuinely good, regardless of personal cost.
question: What does Marcus Aurelius call the undisciplined mind in the Meditations?

options.text: A puppet jerked by every desire
A soldier who has abandoned his post
